pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) THE SMOKING GUN: During a detailed, frantic interview with the family, the mother mentioned that Ms. Vance had been prescribed a prophylactic antibiotic two weeks prior for recurrent urinary tract symptoms, specifically trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim), and had recently started a restrictive keto diet attempting rapid weight loss. Both sulfur-based drugs and caloric restriction are powerful inducers of the specific enzyme pathway. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: AIP is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by a partial deficiency of the enzyme hydroxymethylbilane synthase (HMBS), also known as porphobilinogen (PBG) deaminase. This deficiency is usually clinically latent until an acute attack is triggered by specific stressors (such as fasting, infection, or inducing medications like sulfonamides, barbiturates, or oral contraceptives). When the pathway is induced, the deficient enzyme cannot keep up, leading to a massive accumulation of the neurotoxic precursors delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) and PBG in the liver and circulation. These molecules are responsible for the clinical triad: severe abdominal pain (due to autonomic neuropathy of the gut), psychiatric/neurological symptoms (encephalopathy, seizures, psychosis), and peripheral neuropathy (often mimicking GBS). The profound hyponatremia is often seen due to inappropriate secretion of ADH (SIADH), driven by hypothalamic involvement from the disease process. Definitive diagnosis is made by finding dramatically elevated levels of ALA and PBG in the urine during an acute attack. Treatment involves prompt cessation of the offending trigger and immediate administration of intravenous heme (Hemin/Panhematin) to suppress ALA synthase activity, thereby halting the overproduction of the toxic precursors.
